Title: Strengthening Industry-Academia Collaboration: Enhancing Continuous Professional Development for Engineers
Abstract: In an era marked by accelerated technological change and digital transformation, the need for continuous professional development among engineers has become more urgent than ever. The World Economic Forum's 2023 report forecasts that 50 % of all employees will require reskilling by 2025, with the engineering sector experiencing particularly high pressure to adapt. More than 70 % of engineers are expected to upskill in emerging areas, including artificial intelligence, sustainable engineering, data analytics, and advanced manufacturing technologies. Despite this pressing demand, a persistent gap remains between the competencies developed through academic programs and the practical skills required by industry. This disconnect poses a significant challenge to both educational institutions and employers seeking to prepare and retain a future-ready engineering workforce. This paper explores the potential of industry-academia collaboration as a strategic mechanism to bridge this gap and enhance the continuous professional development of engineering students. By analyzing a series of case studies and existing partnership models, the research examines the effectiveness of collaborative initiatives, including structured internship programs, co-designed curricula, joint research projects, and faculty-industry exchanges. These initiatives are shown to enrich students' learning experiences by integrating real-world challenges into academic settings, thereby fostering a more holistic and practice-oriented educational approach. Findings indicate that such partnerships significantly improve students' disciplinary competencies, critical thinking, and problem-solving abilities. Graduates emerging from programs with strong industry ties demonstrate a higher degree of professional readiness, adaptability, and alignment with current technological and business trends. Furthermore, sustained collaboration helps academic institutions stay abreast of evolving industry standards while enabling companies to influence talent development. Ultimately, the paper argues that a deepening alliance between industry and academia is not only beneficial but essential for cultivating a responsive, innovative, and competitive engineering workforce capable of thriving in the dynamic landscape of the 21st century.
Authors: Christian E. Coronado Mondragon; L. H. González-Guerra; M. Y. Burgos-López; Pedro Perez-Murueta; Víctor Manuel de la Cueva Hernández
Venue: 2025 World Engineering Education Forum - Global Engineering Deans Council (WEEF-GEDC)

Title: Regression Testing Goals - View of Practitioners and Researchers
Abstract: Context: Regression testing is a well-researched area. However, the majority regression testing techniques proposed by the researchers are not getting the attention of the practitioners. Communication gaps between industry and academia, and disparity in the regression testing goals are the main reasons. Close collaboration can help in bridging the communication gaps and resolving the disparities. Objective: The study aims at exploring the views of academics and practitioners about the goals of regression testing. The purpose is to investigate the commonalities and differences in their viewpoints and defining some common goals for the success of regression testing. Method: We conducted a focus group study, with 7 testing experts from industry and academia. 4 testing practitioners from 2 companies and 3 researchers from 2 universities participated in the study. We followed GQM approach, to elicit the regression testing goals, information needs, and measures. Results: 43 regression testing goals were identified by the participants, which were reduced to 10 on the basis of similarity among the identified goals. Later during the priority assignment process, 5 goals were discarded, because the priority assigned to these goals was very low. Participants identified 47 information needs/questions required to evaluate the success of regression testing with reference to goal G5 (confidence). Which were then reduced to 10 on the basis of similarity. Finally, we identified measures to gauge those information needs/questions, which were corresponding to the goal (G5). Conclusions: We observed that participation level of practitioners and researchers during the elicitation of goals and questions was same. We found a certain level of agreement between the participants regarding the regression testing definitions and goals. But there was some level of disagreement regarding the priorities of the goals. We also identified the need to implement a regression testing evaluation framework in the participating companies.
Authors: Nasir Mehmood Minhas; K. Petersen; N. Ali; K. Wnuk

Title: Special issue on collaboration in software testing between industry and academia
Industry-academia collaboration in software testing: An overview of TAIC PART 2015
Abstract: Collaboration between industry and academia in software testing leads to improvement and innovation in industry, and it is the basis for achieving transferable and empirically evaluated results. Thus, the aim of TAIC PART is to forge collaboration between industry and academia on the challenging and exciting problem of real-world software testing. The workshop is promoted by representatives of both industry and academia, bringing together industrial software engineers and testers with researchers working on theory and practice of software testing. We present an overview of the 12th Workshop on Testing: Academia-Industry Collaboration, Practice and Research Techniques (TAIC PART 2017) and its contributions.
Authors: Takashi Kitamura; Emil Alégroth; Rudolf Ramler
Venue: International Conference on Software Testing, Verification and Validation Workshops
